description: The Western Honey Bee is the most widely managed honey-producing bee species in the world. It has a golden-brown body with dark stripes and is valued for its high honey yields and strong pollination abilities. This species plays a major role in agriculture and commercial beekeeping.
behavior: Western Honey Bees live in highly organized colonies with a single queen, thousands of workers, and seasonal drones. They communicate food sources through the waggle dance and defend their hive collectively. Colonies can grow very large during productive nectar seasons.
preferences: This species prefers temperate climates with abundant flowering plants throughout the season. It thrives in orchards, meadows, forests, and managed apiaries. Western Honey Bees are especially attracted to clover, lavender, sunflower, and wildflower blooms.

description: The Italian Honey Bee is one of the most popular honey bee subspecies among beekeepers due to its gentle temperament and strong honey production. It is recognized by its bright yellow and golden coloration. This bee adapts well to managed hives and is commonly used in commercial apiaries.
behavior: Italian Honey Bees are calm, productive, and relatively easy to manage. They maintain large brood populations during warm seasons and actively forage for nectar over long distances. Colonies are less defensive than many other honey bee varieties.
preferences: This subspecies prefers warm climates and regions with long flowering seasons. It performs best in areas rich with nectar-producing plants and reliable water sources. Italian Honey Bees commonly forage on citrus blossoms, clover, eucalyptus, and fruit trees.

description: The Carniolan Honey Bee is a dark-colored honey bee subspecies prized for its calm nature and rapid spring population growth. It is native to parts of Central and Eastern Europe. Beekeepers value this bee for its efficient honey gathering and winter survival abilities.
behavior: Carniolan Honey Bees are known for being gentle and easy to handle. They quickly adjust colony size based on food availability and are efficient foragers in cooler weather. These bees are also recognized for their strong homing instincts.
preferences: This bee prefers temperate and cooler climates with seasonal flowering cycles. It thrives in forested regions, mountain valleys, and agricultural landscapes. Carniolan Honey Bees are attracted to wildflowers, fruit blossoms, and flowering herbs.

description: The Buckfast Bee is a hybrid honey bee developed by selective breeding for productivity and disease resistance. It is widely appreciated by beekeepers for its calm temperament and reliable honey yields. This bee adapts well to different climates and hive conditions.
behavior: Buckfast Bees are active foragers with strong colony organization and low aggression levels. They are less likely to swarm compared to many other honey bee strains. Colonies remain productive throughout long nectar seasons.
preferences: This hybrid species prefers stable hive environments with consistent nectar availability. It performs well in both mild and cooler climates. Buckfast Bees commonly forage on heather, clover, wildflowers, and flowering crops.

description: The Caucasian Honey Bee is known for its long tongue, which allows it to gather nectar from deep flowers. It has a dark gray appearance and produces high-quality honey under suitable conditions. This subspecies is valued for its gentle behavior and strong propolis production.
behavior: Caucasian Honey Bees are calm and relatively non-aggressive toward beekeepers. They forage efficiently in cool and damp weather conditions and can work during light rain or fog. Colonies tend to use large amounts of propolis inside the hive.
preferences: This bee prefers mountainous and cooler regions with diverse flowering plants. It thrives in environments with long nectar flows and moderate humidity. Caucasian Honey Bees are especially attracted to red clover, berry blossoms, and forest flowers.

description: The Russian Honey Bee is a hardy honey bee strain known for its resistance to pests and harsh winter conditions. It was introduced into commercial beekeeping for its natural tolerance to varroa mites. This bee produces moderate to high honey yields when conditions are favorable.
behavior: Russian Honey Bees are active and adaptable foragers that respond quickly to environmental changes. They maintain smaller winter colonies and expand rapidly when nectar becomes available. Colonies can be more defensive than Italian bees but are highly resilient.
preferences: This bee prefers cooler climates with distinct seasonal changes and diverse flowering sources. It performs well in rural farmlands, forests, and managed apiaries. Russian Honey Bees commonly forage on clover, buckwheat, wildflowers, and flowering shrubs.

description: The German Black Bee is one of the oldest honey bee subspecies in Europe and is recognized for its dark body coloration and strong winter endurance. It has historically been important in northern European beekeeping. This bee is valued for its ability to survive in harsh climates.
behavior: German Black Bees are defensive but highly resilient and efficient at conserving resources during winter. They forage steadily even in cooler temperatures and maintain compact colonies. Colonies can become protective when disturbed frequently.
preferences: This subspecies prefers northern and temperate climates with long winters and short flowering seasons. It thrives in forests, rural meadows, and traditional apiaries. German Black Bees are attracted to heather, wild berries, and native woodland flowers.

description: The Cordovan Honey Bee is a selectively bred honey bee strain known for its bright golden coloration and gentle temperament. It is closely related to Italian honey bees and is commonly kept by hobbyist beekeepers. This bee is appreciated for its calm handling characteristics.
behavior: Cordovan Honey Bees are peaceful and easy to inspect within managed hives. They are active nectar collectors and maintain strong colony populations during productive seasons. Colonies generally show low aggression toward humans.
preferences: This strain prefers warm environments with long flowering periods and consistent nectar availability. It thrives in suburban gardens, orchards, and managed apiaries. Cordovan Honey Bees frequently forage on clover, citrus blossoms, and flowering herbs.

description: The African Honey Bee is a highly adaptable honey bee subspecies native to sub-Saharan Africa. It is recognized for its resilience, rapid colony growth, and strong survival instincts. This bee contributes significantly to honey production in tropical regions.
behavior: African Honey Bees are extremely active and highly defensive when protecting their hive. They swarm frequently and quickly establish new colonies in suitable environments. Colonies respond rapidly to threats and environmental changes.
preferences: This bee prefers tropical and subtropical climates with year-round flowering plants. It thrives in savannas, forests, and agricultural regions with abundant nectar sources. African Honey Bees commonly forage on acacia flowers, wild shrubs, and tropical blossoms.

description: The Cape Honey Bee is a unique honey bee subspecies native to South Africa and known for its unusual reproductive traits. It produces quality honey and adapts well to coastal climates. This bee is studied extensively for its genetic characteristics.
behavior: Cape Honey Bees are intelligent foragers and highly adaptive colony builders. Workers are capable of producing female offspring under certain conditions, which is rare among honey bees. Colonies can become competitive when resources are limited.
preferences: This subspecies prefers Mediterranean-style climates with mild winters and seasonal blooms. It thrives in coastal vegetation, shrublands, and flowering agricultural regions. Cape Honey Bees commonly forage on fynbos flowers and flowering fruit crops.

description: The Syrian Honey Bee is a heat-tolerant honey bee subspecies native to the Middle East. It is well adapted to dry climates and scarce floral conditions. Beekeepers value this bee for its persistence and ability to forage during hot weather.
behavior: Syrian Honey Bees are active foragers that begin collecting nectar early in the morning to avoid extreme midday heat. Colonies are energetic and capable of surviving difficult environmental conditions. They can become defensive when hive resources are threatened.
preferences: This bee prefers hot and arid climates with drought-resistant flowering plants. It thrives in rocky landscapes, dry valleys, and cultivated farmlands. Syrian Honey Bees commonly forage on thyme, citrus blossoms, and desert wildflowers.

description: The Anatolian Honey Bee is a hardy subspecies native to Turkey and surrounding regions. It is valued for its strong adaptability, disease resistance, and consistent honey production. This bee performs well across varied environmental conditions.
behavior: Anatolian Honey Bees are energetic foragers and capable of surviving sudden weather changes. Colonies are productive and maintain balanced brood populations throughout the season. They can defend the hive effectively against predators and pests.
preferences: This bee prefers mountainous and temperate climates with diverse plant life. It thrives in highlands, agricultural valleys, and forested regions. Anatolian Honey Bees frequently forage on chestnut blossoms, thyme, and wild meadow flowers.

description: The Cyprian Honey Bee is a Mediterranean honey bee subspecies known for its excellent honey production and resistance to some local pests. It has a slender body and strong flight ability. This bee has adapted well to island climates and dry summers.
behavior: Cyprian Honey Bees are energetic and highly alert while foraging and guarding the hive. Colonies grow rapidly during strong nectar flows and respond quickly to environmental disturbances. They are more defensive than many European honey bee strains.
preferences: This subspecies prefers warm Mediterranean climates with long dry summers and mild winters. It thrives in coastal regions, olive groves, and flowering hillsides. Cyprian Honey Bees are attracted to citrus blossoms, thyme, and native Mediterranean herbs.

description: The Yemeni Honey Bee is a small but highly resilient honey bee subspecies adapted to desert and semi-desert environments. It is capable of surviving extreme heat and limited food resources. This bee is important for traditional honey production in the Arabian Peninsula.
behavior: Yemeni Honey Bees are fast-moving and highly active during cooler parts of the day. Colonies adjust quickly to changing environmental conditions and conserve resources during drought periods. They are efficient at locating sparse nectar sources.
preferences: This bee prefers hot desert climates with seasonal flowering shrubs and trees. It thrives in oasis regions, dry mountain valleys, and arid farmlands. Yemeni Honey Bees commonly forage on sidr trees, acacia flowers, and desert herbs.

description: The Giant Honey Bee is one of the largest honey-producing bee species in the world and is native to South and Southeast Asia. It builds massive open-air combs that can contain large quantities of honey. This species is important in wild honey harvesting traditions.
behavior: Giant Honey Bees are highly defensive and protect their exposed nests aggressively. Colonies migrate seasonally to follow flowering cycles and favorable weather conditions. These bees are powerful long-distance foragers.
preferences: This species prefers tropical forests, cliffs, and tall tree canopies with abundant seasonal blooms. It thrives in warm environments with rich biodiversity. Giant Honey Bees commonly forage on tropical fruit blossoms and forest wildflowers.

description: The Asian Honey Bee is a smaller honey-producing bee species widely kept across Asia for traditional beekeeping. It is naturally resistant to several pests and adapts well to local environmental conditions. This species produces moderate amounts of honey while providing effective pollination.
behavior: Asian Honey Bees are agile foragers with strong defensive behaviors against predators such as hornets. Colonies are smaller than those of Western Honey Bees but are highly efficient in resource management. They swarm readily during favorable seasons.
preferences: This species prefers tropical and subtropical regions with diverse native vegetation. It thrives in forests, rural villages, and mountainous agricultural areas. Asian Honey Bees frequently forage on fruit blossoms, tea flowers, and tropical wild plants.
